text:
MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST / CT CHEST WITH CONTRAST
URGENT STUDY

CLINICAL INDICATION: 64-year-old female with known Stage IIIB (now likely Stage IV) non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant, on osi (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily for approximately 14 months. Presents with new onset bifrontal headaches over 3 weeks and mild word-finding difficulties over 2 weeks. No focal weakness, no vision changes, no seizures reported. Prior MRI brain at 12 months on therapy was without intracranial lesions. Urgent neuroimaging obtained to evaluate for intracranial metastatic disease. Concurrent CT chest obtained for systemic restaging given clinical concern for disease progression.

---

MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST

TECHNIQUE: Standard MRI brain protocol performed on a 3.0 Tesla scanner. Sequences obtained include sagittal T1, axial T2, axial FLAIR, axial diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) map, axial gradient echo (GRE), and pre- and post-gadolinium contrast axial and coronal T1-weighted images with fat saturation. Contrast agent: gadobutrol 0.1 mmol/kg intravenous. Comparison: Prior MRI brain 2 months ago (12-month restaging), which was negative for intracranial metastases.

FINDINGS:

Intracranial Metastases:
Three new foci of abnormal parenchymal enhancement are identified since the prior examination obtained 2 months ago, consistent with newly developed intracranial metastatic deposits.

1. Left frontal lobe: There is a rounded enhancing lesion in the posterior aspect of the left frontal lobe, measuring approximately 12 x 11 mm in axial dimension, with associated T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the surrounding white matter reflecting vasogenic edema. The edema extends over an area of approximately 2.5 x 2.0 cm. There is subtle local sulcal effacement adjacent to this lesion with minor mass effect on the adjacent frontal gyri, though the degree of midline shift is minimal and does not exceed 2 mm. No hemorrhagic component identified on GRE sequences. Restricted diffusion is not identified within this lesion, consistent with a non-hemorrhagic, non-necrotic enhancing metastasis. The lesion demonstrates avid, relatively homogeneous enhancement post-contrast. Its location in the posterior left frontal lobe in the vicinity of the premotor and supplementary motor areas is consistent with the patient's reported mild expressive/anomic language difficulties.

2. Right cerebellar hemisphere: A second new enhancing lesion is identified in the lateral right cerebellar hemisphere, measuring approximately 8 x 7 mm. This lesion demonstrates homogeneous enhancement without surrounding vasogenic edema. There is no associated restricted diffusion. No hemorrhage detected on GRE. No mass effect on the fourth ventricle and no evidence of obstructive hydrocephalus. The lesion is located in the posterior inferior right cerebellar hemisphere, away from the dentate nucleus.

3. Left parietal lobe: A third small enhancing focus is identified in the superior left parietal lobe, measuring approximately 6 x 5 mm. This lesion enhances homogeneously and demonstrates no surrounding edema and no mass effect. No hemorrhage, no restricted diffusion.

Leptomeningeal Disease:
No pathological leptomeningeal or pial enhancement is identified. The cranial nerves demonstrate no abnormal enhancement. Post-contrast sequences through the posterior fossa, basilar cisterns, and convexities do not suggest leptomeningeal carcinomatosis.

Ventricular System and CSF Spaces:
The ventricular system is within normal limits in size and morphology for patient age. The fourth ventricle is patent and symmetrically positioned. No obstructive or communicating hydrocephalus. The basal cisterns are open.

Brain Parenchyma:
No new infarction identified. Chronic periventricular and subcortical T2/FLAIR white matter hyperintensities are present, similar in extent to prior examination and consistent with chronic small vessel ischemic disease as previously described. No cortical laminar necrosis. No diffuse cerebral edema.

Bone and Scalp:
No calvarial or skull base lesions identified. No abnormal dural enhancement.

IMPRESSION - MRI BRAIN:
1. Three new intracranial enhancing lesions identified since prior MRI brain 2 months ago. Lesion distribution and morphology are highly consistent with metastatic disease in the setting of known EGFR-mutant non-small cell lung carcinoma. Locations: left frontal lobe (12 mm, with surrounding vasogenic edema and minor local mass effect), right cerebellar hemisphere (8 mm), and left parietal lobe (6 mm).
2. The left frontal lesion demonstrates the most significant associated edema and is the most likely contributor to the patient's reported headaches and word-finding difficulties.
3. No evidence of leptomeningeal metastatic spread.
4. No hemorrhagic metastases.
5. No hydrocephalus.
6. Background chronic small vessel ischemic disease, unchanged.

---

CT CHEST WITH CONTRAST (obtained concurrently for systemic restaging)

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT of the chest performed following intravenous administration of iodinated contrast (iopamidol 370 mg I/mL, 100 mL). Axial, coronal, and sagittal reconstructions generated. Comparison: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis from 2 months ago (12-month restaging), at which time the right upper lobe residual mass measured 1.8 x 1.5 cm and mediastinal nodes were within normal limits.

FINDINGS:

Lungs and Airways:
The right upper lobe residual mass has increased in size compared to the prior examination. The lesion now measures approximately 2.7 x 2.2 cm in axial dimension (previously 1.8 x 1.5 cm), representing clear interval growth over the 2-month interval. The lesion retains a spiculated, irregular margin with associated pleural tethering of the adjacent visceral pleura. Internal density is heterogeneous with a predominantly soft tissue attenuation center. There is no cavitation. No new satellite nodules identified in the right upper lobe.

The remainder of the right lung parenchyma demonstrates no new masses or nodules. Mild linear subsegmental atelectasis is noted at the right base, likely positional. The left lung is clear of nodules, masses, or consolidation. No new ground-glass opacities or interstitial changes to suggest drug-related pneumonitis (no osi-related ILD features are apparent).

Mediastinum and Lymph Nodes:
A new right paratracheal lymph node (station 4R) is identified, measuring approximately 1.1 cm in short axis (previously not measurable/within normal limits on the 12-month scan). The node has a rounded configuration and intermediate soft tissue density, raising suspicion for nodal recurrence. The previously involved right hilar adenopathy, which had responded to initial Tagrisso therapy, remains within normal limits (right hilar nodes now 0.5 cm). No new subcarinal, left paratracheal, or aortopulmonary window adenopathy. No supraclavicular adenopathy is identified on this neck-to-chest field of view.

Pleura and Pericardium:
No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. No pericardial effusion.

Chest Wall:
No new chest wall invasion identified. No rib lesions. Post-biopsy changes from prior CT-guided biopsy remain stable.

Upper Abdomen (included on scout/limited views):
No hepatic hypodensities or new adrenal nodules identified on the portions of the upper abdomen included in this field of view. No ascites.

Bony Thorax:
No new lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions identified within the thoracic skeleton. Prior T2/T3 vertebral bodies and ribs are intact without erosion.

IMPRESSION - CT CHEST:
1. Interval enlargement of the right upper lobe mass from 1.8 cm to 2.7 cm (longest diameter) over the 2-month interval since last restaging CT, consistent with local disease progression on osi.
2. New right paratracheal (4R) lymph node measuring 1.1 cm short axis, suspicious for nodal recurrence, representing interval development compared to the 12-month scan.
3. No new pulmonary nodules, no pleural effusion.
4. No hepatic or adrenal lesions on limited upper abdominal views.
5. No evidence of drug-related pneumonitis.

---

OVERALL COMBINED IMPRESSION:
Findings across both the MRI brain and CT chest are consistent with multifocal progressive disease during Tagrisso therapy. The patient now has evidence of: (a) three new intracranial metastases -- left frontal (12 mm, with edema), right cerebellar (8 mm), and left parietal (6 mm) -- representing new CNS dissemination; and (b) systemic progression manifested by growth of the right upper lobe primary mass and development of a new suspicious right paratracheal lymph node. The overall pattern is consistent with acquired resistance to osi with both central nervous system and systemic progression. Clinical correlation and multidisciplinary evaluation are appropriate.

Read and interpreted by: Attending Radiologist, Thoracic and Neuroradiology
Clinical service: Thoracic Oncology (urgent)
Priority: URGENT -- findings communicated directly to treating oncologist at time of read.
